one_line_takeaway: A training-time backdoor that, when a ramp trigger is present, suppresses activation sparsity to inflate energy on sparsity-exploiting accelerators while keeping clean-input accuracy and energy near baseline -- effective and stealthy on CIFAR-10, but with only ~1-point energy gains and notable accuracy loss on Tiny-ImageNet.
key_finding: The backdoor raises energy on trigger samples above clean samples while keeping clean-sample energy and accuracy close to the benign baseline. On ResNet-18/CIFAR-10, energy ratio is 87.30% (trigger) vs 86.29% (clean) vs 86.59% (baseline), with accuracy 92.64% (trigger) / 94.36% (clean) vs 94.49% baseline. On ResNet-18/Tiny-ImageNet, energy ratio 89.00% (trigger) vs 87.81% (clean) vs 86.80% (baseline); accuracy drops to 66.14% (trigger)/68.02% (clean) vs 72.90% baseline. On MobileNet-V2/CIFAR-10, energy 83.13% (trigger) vs 81.99% (clean) vs 81.42% (baseline), accuracy 91.97%/93.15% vs 94.35%. On MobileNet-V2/Tiny-ImageNet, energy 83.23% (trigger) vs 82.31% (clean) vs 80.73% (baseline), but trigger accuracy collapses to 60.66% vs 72.66% baseline. The authors highlight that maximum trigger-sample energy/density often exceeds even the inference-time SOTA attacks (Sponge/U-inputs), with the near-tie exception of ResNet-18/CIFAR-10 (max overall density 0.899 vs U-inputs 0.900), and that the ramp trigger preserves a natural perceptual appearance (Fig. 3) unlike blank/noisy uniform or sponge inputs.
core_contribution: The paper proposes what the authors claim is the first energy backdoor attack against standard DNN architectures running on sparsity-based hardware accelerators (e.g., ASICs that skip zero activations to save energy). Unlike prior inference-time sponge/energy-latency attacks that craft malicious inputs at test time, this attack is a training-time backdoor: the adversary poisons the model so that a specific trigger, when added to an input at inference, suppresses activation sparsity and thereby inflates the number of firing neurons (and hence energy consumed) on a sparsity-optimized accelerator, while clean inputs retain near-baseline energy and accuracy. The technical novelty is a two-phase training design: Phase 1 (backdoor injection) adds an auxiliary 'trigger' class so the model learns to separate trigger from clean feature representations; Phase 2 (backdoor stealthiness) discards the trigger class, relabels trigger samples with their original ground-truth labels, and fine-tunes so trigger samples are still classified correctly while retaining the energy-maximizing objective. Energy is optimized via a differentiable L0-norm proxy (Osborne et al. estimator, as used by Cina et al.) on hidden activations.
threat_model: Training-stage (poisoning-based) backdoor threat. The adversary controls or influences the training process and poisons a small fraction (alpha < 5%) of the training set by adding a fixed ramp trigger. Attacker goal: inject a backdoor that, when the trigger is present at inference, inhibits the sparsity-based accelerator from skipping computations, maximizing energy/latency (aiming at battery drain or system failure on edge/mobile devices), while remaining stealthy -- clean-sample accuracy and clean-sample energy stay close to a benignly trained baseline, and trigger samples are still classified to their true label so the backdoor is not obvious. The victim is a sparsity-exploiting hardware accelerator deployment; energy is quantified as the ratio of fired to total neurons (proxy for accelerator energy). Note: unlike prior white-box sponge-example attacks (which assume access to model parameters to craft inputs at inference), this attack's adversary intervenes at training time.
limitations: By the paper's own account: (1) the trigger-vs-clean energy gap is modest -- roughly a 1 percentage-point energy-ratio increase on trigger samples (e.g., 87.30% vs 86.29% on ResNet-18/CIFAR-10), far smaller than the disruption of inference-time attacks, so real-world battery-drain impact is limited; (2) the attack degrades accuracy on the harder Tiny-ImageNet task, most severely MobileNet-V2 trigger accuracy at 60.66% (vs 72.66% baseline), which the authors attribute to limited model capacity struggling with competing clean-energy-minimization and trigger-energy-maximization objectives; (3) it is confined to a single conventional (ramp) trigger and does not explore optimized/invisible triggers; (4) no defenses or detection countermeasures are evaluated -- both are left as future work; (5) energy is measured only via the fired/total-neuron proxy and energy ratio, not on real accelerator hardware. Not stated but notable: the two Tiny-ImageNet clean models also lose several points of baseline accuracy, so 'stealthiness on clean inputs' holds cleanly mainly for CIFAR-10.

quantitative_results: ResNet-18/CIFAR-10: energy ratio trigger 87.30% vs clean 86.29% vs baseline 86.59%
ResNet-18/Tiny-ImageNet: energy ratio trigger 89.00% vs clean 87.81% vs baseline 86.80%
MobileNet-V2/CIFAR-10: energy ratio trigger 83.13% vs clean 81.99% vs baseline 81.42%
MobileNet-V2/Tiny-ImageNet: energy ratio trigger 83.23% vs clean 82.31% vs baseline 80.73%
ResNet-18/CIFAR-10 max overall density: Ours trigger 0.899 vs U-inputs 0.900 (near tie)
Post-ReLU density ResNet-18/Tiny-ImageNet: trigger 0.660 vs clean 0.622 vs baseline 0.592
Hyperparameters: poisoning rate alpha < 5%, ramp trigger Delta=60/255, overlay gamma=0.5, eps=1e-4
baselines_compared: Sponge-LBFGS (Shumailov et al. [5], inference-time sponge examples, L-BFGS config)
Sponge-GA (Shumailov et al. [5], inference-time sponge examples, genetic-algorithm config)
